mappings.rationaleAI: Japanese literature addresses the character of Japanese civilization (A), Heian literature (B.3.b.i), Kamakura culture (C.1.d), Muromachi (C.3.c), and Tokugawa literature (C.4.c).
The samurai class addresses the rise of samurai (B.3.b.ii), Kamakura bakufu (C.1.a), and Tokugawa class structure (C.4.b).
Meiji Restoration connects to the downfall of the bakuhan system (C.4.d.iii).
Neo-Confucianism connects to Kamakura philosophy (C.1.d), Tokugawa revival (C.4.d.ii), and Yi dynasty Korea (D.5.a).
Buddhism connects to Yamato introduction (B.2.a), Nara flowering (B.3.a), Kamakura Zen (C.1.d), Muromachi influence (C.3.c), and Korea (D.2).
Directly covers the entire Korean section from prehistoric origins through the Yi dynasty.
Mongol invasions of Japan (C.1.c) and Korea (D.4).
Dynastic succession connects to Yamato (B.2), Fujiwara (B.3), shogunates (C.1, C.4), and Yi dynasty (D.5).
Archaeological methods connect to sources (A), prehistoric Japanese cultures (B.1), and Korean origins (D.1).

Covers the spread of Buddhism among Mongol and Himalayan peoples and the Dalai Lama institution, relevant to the introduction of Buddhism in the Yamato court period, Buddhism's flowering in the Nara period, and the influence of Zen Buddhism.

Covers the historical context of European colonial encounters in Southeast Asia including Portuguese and Spanish activities, providing comparative context for the arrival of the Portuguese and Spanish in Japan in the 1540s.
The Reformation's global spread through missionary activity including Catholic Jesuit missions directly addresses the Catholic Jesuit missionary activity in Japan beginning in 1549.
